projects.title: Drone Project
projects.description:
The Aviation Club is developing a multipurpose quadrotor drone to serve as a medical response proof-of-concept and a platform for machine learning research. During the initial design phase, the team ran simulations to verify component compatibility, flight time, and payload capacity. Members created a custom CAD model to calculate the center of gravity and ensure the drone could modularly accept different payloads. To fund manufacturing, club officers presented to the PTSA and secured a grant of over 700 dollars. The drone was assembled using a combination of outsourced carbon fiber parts and components manufactured using the high school's 3D printers.  

This is an active, ongoing project. As of the summer of 2026, the team is currently around stage three and moving into stage four. Stage three focuses on initial flight tests to safely tune the flight controller and make necessary hardware and software modifications. The club plans to request authorization from school administrators to conduct these test flights on campus without a payload. As the team advances into the final application phase, the project will likely be completed by 2027.  

Once the platform is deemed safe for use, the team will proceed with its stage four applications. The first objective tests a medical configuration designed to provide real-time injury assessments using AI diagnostic systems and deliver medication via a custom attachment. The second objective utilizes the drone for machine learning research, specifically evaluating how real flight telemetry and camera distortions impact the accuracy of object detection models.

projects.title: L1 Rocket
projects.description:
The Aviation Club designed and built a rocket for the 2025 American Rocketry Challenge. The objective was to launch two eggs to an altitude of 750 feet and return them safely within 36 to 39 seconds. Members collaborated on the design and assembled the rocket themselves, which included laser-cutting the parts. Once completed, a group of club officers drove three hours to the LUNAR launch site at Snow Ranch for testing.

The first test run was a success. For recovery, the rocket was designed to descend in two sections: one containing the engine, and the upper section housing the altimeter and the two eggs. Unfortunately, during the second run, the entire upper section containing both the altimeter and the eggs was lost. Because the competition requires two successful runs to qualify, the team was disqualified. A later review identified several issues throughout the project, including parachute problems caused by misreading the rules, an altered center of gravity due to mistakenly using cooked eggs instead of raw ones, delays in purchasing parts, and delays in collecting signed competition forms from all members. The team will use these setbacks to improve future design planning.

siteContent.homeHeroSubtitle: Los Altos High School Aviation Club. Designing drones, rockets, planes, and inspiring students
siteContent.missionStatement: Bridging the gap between the theoretical and real-world engineering through hands-on fabrication and flight testing.
siteContent.meetingsSubtitle: Workshops, launches, activities, simulations, and more.
siteContent.projectsSubtitle: Current engineering efforts in rocketry, fixed-wing aircraft, and multi-rotor systems.
siteContent.contactSubtitle: We are looking for students passionate about engineering, design, and flight. No prior experience is required—just curiosity.
siteContent.marqueeText: Welcome to the Los Altos High School Aviation Club • Join us every Tuesday in Room 702 • Engineering the future of flight • Blue Skies Ahead

pillars.title: Avionics
pillars.description: Development of custom flight computers, telemetry systems, and autonomous control logic for UAVs.

pillars.title: Aerodynamics
pillars.description: Utilizing CFD analysis and wind tunnel testing to optimize airframe efficiency and stability.

pillars.title: Crew Training
pillars.description: Mentorship programs pairing senior engineers with new members to teach CAD, soldering, and piloting.

featureBoxes.title: Design
featureBoxes.description: Learn the principles of aeronautics and use CAD software to design your own airframes.

featureBoxes.title: Build
featureBoxes.description: Get hands-on experience with composite materials, electronics, and mechanical assembly.

featureBoxes.title: Fly
featureBoxes.description: Take your creations to the skies and analyze flight data to improve performance.
